rights.basis: The item and collection rights layer state Public Domain / No Copyright-United States and provide educational and research access. Provider terms continue to govern the interface and service.
rights.component: University of Michigan electronic-text item

entities.description: Public electronic text representing Löweneck's work title page and the English/right-hand text on printed odd pp. 3-53. It omits the surviving German introduction, facing Latin comparisons, annotations pp. 54-57, and advertisements.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00117

rights.basis: The item and collection rights layer state Public Domain / No Copyright-United States and provide educational and research access. Provider terms continue to govern the interface and service.
rights.component: University of Michigan electronic-text item

dateStatements.label: Translation and compilation
dateStatements.notes: The exact date is disputed; this is a work date, not the 1160-1199 date of the surviving witness.
dateStatements.value: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century

entities.description: The anonymous post-Conquest Old English prose medical translation and compilation: a short historical and theoretical epistle followed by clinical and therapeutic remedies organized a capite ad calcem. The sole surviving copy is incomplete at the end. This abstract work layer is distinct from its manuscript, editions, digital encodings, and images.
entities.label: Peri Didaxeon (Old English medical work)
notes: Extant only in an imperfect copy; no complete ending is known.

rights.basis: The work is directly attested in a late-twelfth-century manuscript, and the current U-M item is marked Public Domain / No Copyright-United States. This statement concerns the medieval wording, not manuscript photographs, modern catalog prose, modern scholarship, digital interfaces, or repository markup.
rights.component: Late-eleventh-/early-twelfth-century Old English and embedded Latin textual work

dateStatements.label: Oldest cited witness
dateStatements.notes: This attestation predates the twelfth-century physician Petrocello and makes the traditional attribution doubtful.
dateStatements.value: Mid-ninth century in Paris, BnF lat. 11219

entities.description: The older Latin medical text branch transmitted by Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, lat. 11219 (L), British Library Sloane 2839 (S), and British Library Harley 4977 (H). Maion names it Tereoperica from the title in the oldest witness and shows that Peri Didaxeon aligns closely with this branch. It is distinct from De Renzi's R/V/P branch edition and from a proven direct exemplar for the Old English translator.
entities.label: Tereoperica Latin medical textual tradition

rights.basis: Maion identifies a mid-ninth-century witness to the Latin branch. This conclusion concerns the medieval Latin wording, not modern editions, transcriptions, catalogs, or images.
rights.component: Medieval Latin textual work and textual tradition

bibliographic.citation: Max Löweneck, ed., Peri Didaxeon: Eine Sammlung von Rezepten in englischer Sprache aus dem 11./12. Jahrhundert. Nach einer Handschrift des Britischen Museums, Erlanger Beiträge zur englischen Philologie 12 (Erlangen: Fr. Junge, 1896).
bibliographic.title: Peri Didaxeon: Eine Sammlung von Rezepten in englischer Sprache aus dem 11./12. Jahrhundert

entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition represented by U-M and CME00117. Löweneck transcribed Harley MS 6258 B directly, moved manuscript marginal notes and glosses into footnotes, marked Cockayne's divergent readings C., and printed Latin comparison passages opposite the English text.
entities.label: Löweneck's 1896 Peri Didaxeon

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 57 printed pages, plus series matter and advertisements in the scanned bound volume
notes: The edition prints Latin comparison passages on facing even pages and the English text on odd pages.
The available scanned exemplar lacks printed introduction pp. i-v even though the edition's bibliographic extent is viii + 57.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1896, and both the current U-M item and Internet Archive item describe the represented publication as public-domain/no-copyright material. This does not determine rights in later scans, provider metadata, or interfaces.
rights.component: Löweneck's 1896 edition text, introduction, and apparatus

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Oswald Cockayne, ed., Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, vol. 3, Rolls Series 35 (London, 1866), Peri Didaxeon at pp. 81-145.
bibliographic.pages: Peri Didaxeon at pp. 81-145; Old English text begins p. 82
bibliographic.title: Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England

entities.description: Cockayne's earlier manuscript-based edition in Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft. Löweneck did not silently copy it as his base text: he says his own edition rests directly on Harley MS 6258 B and records Cockayne's divergent readings in footnotes with C.
entities.label: Cockayne's 1866 first printed Peri Didaxeon text
notes: The complete three-volume series spans 1864-1866; Peri Didaxeon is specifically in volume III (1866), with its sectional title on p. 81, Old English text beginning on p. 82, and the final Old English and English text on pp. 144-145.
Internet Archive's item metadata supplies the series start date 1864, but direct inspection of the cached volume-III title page establishes 1866 for this edition entity.

Cockayne's opening header reads 'MS. Harl. 6258. fol. 83 b.=42 b.' at printed p. 82 (PDF p. 124; BookReader n123), and his text later marks fol. 98 b. at printed p. 142 before ending on p. 144. The current British Library catalog instead locates the same target at ff. 51v-66v. These are retained as target-specific historical/current endpoint layers; no whole-codex foliation conversion is inferred.

rights.basis: The cached title page establishes publication of volume III in 1866. This conclusion concerns Cockayne's historical edition layer, not later scans, provider metadata, or interfaces.
rights.component: Cockayne's 1866 volume-III edition text, translation, notes, and apparatus

bibliographic.citation: Salvatore De Renzi, ed., Collectio Salernitana, ossia Documenti inediti, e trattati di medicina appartenenti alla scuola medica salernitana, vol. 4 (Naples: Tipografia del Filiatre-Sebezio, 1856), 185-286.
bibliographic.title: Collectio Salernitana

entities.description: The Latin comparison edition used primarily for Löweneck's facing text. De Renzi edited the R witness, Paris BnF lat. 14025, in Collectio Salernitana IV. Maion places R with V and P in a branch distinct from the older L/S/H Tereoperica branch closest to the Old English work; De Renzi is therefore a comparative printed intermediary, not the Old English translator's direct source.
entities.label: De Renzi's 1856 Practica Petrocelli edition
notes: Internet Archive's item metadata supplies the series start date 1852, but direct inspection of the cached volume-IV title page establishes 1856 for this edition entity.

rights.basis: The cached title page establishes publication of volume IV in 1856. This conclusion concerns De Renzi's historical edition layer, not later scans, provider metadata, or interfaces.
rights.component: De Renzi's 1856 volume-IV edition text, commentary, and apparatus

rights.basis: The current catalog states that its text is © British Library Board and CC BY except where otherwise stated. This catalog-data layer is distinct from the manuscript and its images.
rights.component: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog descriptive text

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: Witness production date, not work composition date.

entities.description: Late-twelfth-century English parchment medical miscellany. Peri Didaxeon is its third main work, at ff. 51v-66v and more exactly f. 51v/23-f. 66v/23. The text breaks off and a lower-margin catchword plus gathering number shows that at least one further quire once followed.

holding.notes: The work-level component record is 041-002162301; manuscripts are consulted at the British Library in London subject to registration and advance request.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 6258 B

physicalDescription.extent: v + 66 + v leaves; modern pencil foliation ff. 1-66
notes: Produced in England and written as one codex in Insular minuscule with Caroline and Protothic features; EM1060 identifies one scribe for the target work.
The manuscript is imperfect at the beginning and end, has lost leaves and fire-damaged leaves, and was rebound in 1972.
Peri Didaxeon occupies sixteen folios in the current catalog, ff. 51v-66v.

Cockayne's 1866 edition labels the target opening 'fol. 83 b.=42 b.' and later marks fol. 98 b.; those historical foliation labels crosswalk to the current catalog's target endpoints but are not normalized into a general offset beyond Peri Didaxeon.

rights.basis: Copyright status of the medieval wording does not itself govern custody, reader access, handling, photography, catalog data, or reproductions of the physical object.

entities.description: Danielle Maion's detailed study of the sole manuscript, date, structure, language, practical-medical organization, translation practices, and Latin textual tradition. It corrects Löweneck's treatment of the final catchword and replaces simple dependence on De Renzi's Petrocello attribution with the older L/S/H Tereoperica branch.

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether its legacy viewer or existing microfilm/digital surrogate can be restored or supplied for Harley MS 6258 B.
If ordering images, request the complete present object plus covers and flyleaves and identify ff. 51v-66v separately; request color explicitly if black-and-white microfilm would be insufficient.
After lawful delivery, checksum every file, preserve the invoice and license, and map exact folio, canvas/file, start, and end boundaries before claiming completeness.
notes: No inquiry was sent during this review. Negative search evidence is dated and does not establish destruction or institutional absence.
openQuestions.question: Can the former British Library viewer object or another complete, foliation-stable facsimile of Harley MS 6258 B be restored, supplied, or newly photographed?

next_steps: Consult Maion's 1999 critical edition and subsequent work on the complete Latin tradition.
Compare Peri Didaxeon with Paris BnF lat. 11219, British Library Sloane 2839, and British Library Harley 4977 without assuming any extant witness was the direct exemplar.
Keep Löweneck's De Renzi/Gariopontus/Isidore comparisons, the Tereoperica source family, manuscript date, and southwestern scribal features as distinct evidence layers.
openQuestions.question: Which lost Latin exemplar or intermediary state underlies Peri Didaxeon, and can the work's translation date and place be narrowed beyond probable late-eleventh-/early-twelfth-century England?

next_steps: Locate a second institutional copy or scan containing introduction pp. i-v and compare the entire volume with b30591855.
Continue to label each cached derivative complete for its provider source file while describing the scanned edition portion as missing pp. i-v.
Seek provider clarification before treating either the Public Domain Mark or the conflicting note as a legal warranty for all derivatives.
openQuestions.question: Can a leaf-complete Löweneck 1896 exemplar be found, and what exactly does Internet Archive's item-level Public Domain Mark cover given the metadata note 'title is copyright'?

next_steps: Request written item-specific terms with any image order and preserve them alongside the files.
Ask separately about research reference use, public web redistribution, and print-on-demand reproduction.
Do not transfer the medieval text's public-domain status or the catalog text's CC BY statement to manuscript images.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific rights and delivery conditions would govern British Library images of Harley MS 6258 B for repository storage, public web display, and print-on-demand use?

lineage.summary: CME00117 is the checked-in repository representation of the University of Michigan encoding of Max Löweneck's 1896 Peri Didaxeon. U-M selected Löweneck's title page and English text on printed odd pp. 3-53, omitting his German introduction, facing Latin comparison text, final annotations, and advertisements. Löweneck states that his English edition is based directly on British Library Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v; Cockayne's earlier edition supplied explicitly marked comparison readings, not a hidden transcription layer. Modern scholarship identifies the Old English work as an adaptive version of the older Latin L/S/H branch called Tereoperica, rather than simply a translation of De Renzi's R-branch Practica attributed to Petrocello. The sole manuscript copy is late twelfth century and imperfect at the end. The full currently published Internet Archive PDF/OCR/metadata set for its scanned Löweneck exemplar is cached, but it represents the edition only partially because the exemplar lacks printed introduction pp. i-v; exact tested BookReader boundaries are n8 for the encoded title and n14-n64 for the encoded English text. Complete public provider PDFs and metadata are also cached and bounded for Cockayne volume III (Peri Didaxeon at printed pp. 81-145 / PDF pp. 123-187) and De Renzi volume IV (Practica at printed pp. 185-286 / PDF pp. 193-294). No currently usable legitimate complete public manuscript facsimile was found: the current British Library catalog says images are unavailable, legacy viewer links redirect to a generic page, and plausible IIIF and aggregator searches did not yield an auditable target object.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Old English translator-compiler
catalogSummary.date: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century; the sole surviving copy is late twelfth century

catalogSummary.title: Peri Didaxeon

catalogingSubject.label: Peri Didaxeon
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous post-Conquest Old English medical translation and compilation conventionally called Peri Didaxeon: a short historical and theoretical epistle followed by remedies organized by ailment from head downward. The sole known copy, British Library Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v, is imperfect at the end. Löweneck's 1896 title page and notes, his facing Latin comparison text, the physical manuscript, British Library images, the Internet Archive scan, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML are separate layers. CME00117 encodes Löweneck's title page and English text, not his full German introduction, facing Latin columns, or final annotations; its numbered item 67 is the manuscript's quire catchword rather than a final work chapter.

contentParts.summary: Sixty-five surviving entries organized a capite ad calcem by disease or affected body part, with Latin and occasional Old English headings, clinical signs, pharmacological or regimen remedies, bloodletting, and brief prognoses.

contentParts.label: Manuscript quire catchword represented as item 67

contentParts.summary: The words 'Ȝif þæt blod of þan innoþe cumþ' and Roman numeral vii at the bottom of f. 66v are a catchword and gathering number for a lost following quire, not the opening of a complete final work chapter.

assertion.notes: Maion's dating of the work is later and narrower than Löweneck's obsolete ca. 1035-ca. 1200 range. The exact translation date remains disputed.

dateStatements.display: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century
Late twelfth century (1160-1199) in British Library Harley MS 6258 B
1866 in volume III of Cockayne's Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England, sectional title p. 81 and text pp. 82-145
notes: Löweneck's represented edition followed in 1896 and records Cockayne's divergent readings with C.

Cockayne's transcription identifies its opening as historical fol. 83b (=42b) and later marks fol. 98b; the current British Library locus is ff. 51v-66v. This target-specific crosswalk is not extended into a whole-codex foliation formula.

extent.description: CME00117 contains two DIV1 elements and 67 sequential DIV2 TYPE='item' elements numbered 1-67, with 78 HEAD, 150 MILESTONE, 309 NOTE, 70 P, 27 PB, 1,672 HI, two SUP, and no GAP, UNCLEAR, LG, or L. Sixty-six numbered items contain medieval prose; item 67 is an empty P preceded by the f. 66v quire catchword misrepresented as a work heading. The 27 PB elements represent the modern title page and the edition's English text on printed odd pp. 3-53, not every page of the parent edition.

formStatements.description: A prose medical handbook with a short historical and theoretical epistle followed by practical clinical and therapeutic entries; no verse is encoded.

genres.label: Translation
notes: The source is the older Latin branch Maion calls Tereoperica, not automatically De Renzi's later R-branch text attributed to Petrocello.

languageStatements.label: Old English
notes: The surviving post-Conquest copy mixes late West Saxon with early Middle English and idiosyncratic scribal features. CME00117's TEXT/@LANG='enm' is retained as an encoding label, not silently converted into a claim that the abstract work consists of a separate Middle English language layer.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: This intrinsic bilingual medical register is distinct from Löweneck's facing Latin source comparison, which U-M omitted.

scope.description: Intrinsic rubrics, disease names, medical terms, and formulae within the vernacular work

notes: Description is evidence-layered: the medieval work, sole physical witness, British Library catalog and images, Löweneck edition, Cockayne and De Renzi comparative sources, Internet Archive scan, U-M encoding, and repository XML are not interchangeable.

Foliation is evidence-layered too: Cockayne's historical target labels fol. 83b (=42b) through fol. 98b correspond to current British Library ff. 51v-66v only at the evidenced Peri Didaxeon endpoints; no whole-codex conversion is asserted.
All counts were independently recomputed from the checked-in XML on 2026-07-13.

next_steps: Consult Maion's full 1999 critical edition and current stemmatic scholarship rather than extending Löweneck's Petrocello attribution.

Compare the complete Old English text with Paris BnF lat. 11219, British Library Sloane 2839, and British Library Harley 4977 while distinguishing source-family agreement from direct-exemplar identity.
Keep witness date, witness dialect, work date, and place of translation as separate claims.
openQuestions.question: When and where was Peri Didaxeon translated and compiled, and which lost Latin exemplar or intermediary state underlies its relationship to the L/S/H Tereoperica branch?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the former complete viewer object or an existing microfilm/digital surrogate can be restored or supplied.
If ordering new images, request the complete present manuscript plus covers and flyleaves, and separately identify current ff. 51v-66v; preserve the delivery license and exact folio-to-file map.
Use a foliation-stable surrogate to audit Cockayne's historical fol. 83b (=42b) through fol. 98b labels against current ff. 51v-66v, without assuming a constant offset outside Peri Didaxeon.
Do not bypass the provider or infer canvas boundaries from inaccessible legacy URLs.
notes: Negative web evidence is dated, not proof that no institutional surrogate exists.

The edition/catalog endpoint crosswalk is secure for the target, but exact image-level reconciliation of all intervening historical and current folio labels remains unaudited without a current manuscript surrogate.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete, foliation-stable public or request-delivered color facsimile of Harley MS 6258 B be obtained and mapped to Peri Didaxeon ff. 51v-66v?

next_steps: Locate a second institutional scan or physical copy and compare all preliminaries, text, annotations, and advertisements against b30591855.
Treat the cached PDF as complete for the provider-published file but partial for the edition's originally paginated preliminaries.
Seek provider clarification before treating the metadata note or Public Domain Mark as a warranty for every scan component.
openQuestions.question: Where can a leaf-complete exemplar of Löweneck 1896, including introduction pp. i-v, be inspected, and how should Internet Archive's Public Domain Mark be reconciled with its 'title is copyright' note?

notes: The sole witness was made in England and the work belongs to the English vernacular medical tradition; this does not establish a town or institution of translation.

assertion.notes: Maion identifies late West Saxon features alongside forms characteristic of early Middle English, especially southwestern dialects. This is scribal-language evidence, not proof of the translation place.

scope.description: Linguistic features of the surviving copy

notes: The work is a vernacular version of material from the Latin textual family Maion calls Tereoperica; neither Petrocello nor the author of the Latin tradition is assigned authorship of the Old English wording.

assertion.notes: The translation abbreviates, adds, omits, varies, and reorganizes source material; whether all such interventions originated with the English translator or an earlier Latin compiler cannot be separated completely.

scope.description: Responsibility for the represented 1896 edition, not medieval authorship
Direct transcription from Harley MS 6258 B, ff. 51v-66v
Cockayne's divergent readings, marked C. in the 1896 footnotes

subjects.label: Head-to-foot medical organization
notes: The surviving sequence proceeds a capite ad calcem from head ailments to stomach disorders, where the copy breaks off.

summaries.value: Peri Didaxeon is an anonymous post-Conquest Old English prose medical handbook translated and adapted from an older Latin textual family now called Tereoperica. A brief epistle recounts medical history and theory; the main collection then organizes disease descriptions, diagnoses, prognoses, and predominantly pharmacological remedies from head downward, with some dietetic and bloodletting instructions. Latin rubrics and terminology remain embedded in the vernacular text. The sole late-twelfth-century copy breaks off among stomach disorders. CME00117 represents Max Löweneck's 1896 direct manuscript edition of the English text, with his comparative apparatus, through the University of Michigan encoding.

The XML has one modern edition-title DIV1 and one medical-collection DIV1 containing 67 numbered item DIV2 elements. Item 1 is the historical and theoretical epistle. Items 2-66 contain the surviving sequence of remedies. Item 67 has an empty paragraph and a heading made from the text and Roman numeral at the bottom of f. 66v; Maion identifies that material as a quire catchword and gathering number, showing that another quire once followed.

The U-M/repository text selects Löweneck's title page (PDF p. 9 / BookReader n8) and the English/right-hand text on printed odd pp. 3-53 (PDF pp. 15-65 / BookReader n14-n64). It omits the surviving German introduction pp. vi-viii, all facing Latin/even-page comparisons, final annotations pp. 54-57, and advertisements. The available Internet Archive provider PDF is complete as that published digital file, but its scanned exemplar begins the introduction at printed p. vi and lacks pp. i-v; it must not be described as a leaf-complete copy of every part of the 1896 edition.

titles.supplied_by: Conventional title from the manuscript opening, Löweneck, and the current British Library component catalog

Exact main title and subtitle of the represented 1896 edition

work.summary: Peri Didaxeon is an anonymous post-Conquest Old English prose medical handbook translated and adapted from an older Latin textual family now called Tereoperica. A brief epistle recounts medical history and theory; the main collection then organizes disease descriptions, diagnoses, prognoses, and predominantly pharmacological remedies from head downward, with some dietetic and bloodletting instructions. Latin rubrics and terminology remain embedded in the vernacular text. The sole late-twelfth-century copy breaks off among stomach disorders. CME00117 represents Max Löweneck's 1896 direct manuscript edition of the English text, with his comparative apparatus, through the University of Michigan encoding.
